sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 71-year-old male, presented with a 3-month history of cough, initially noticed while loading horses, which he attributes to strenuous activity.
He had a cold that resolved, and his cough has been intermittent since then.
He has a history of bronchitis diagnosed by his primary care physician and a lung nodule found on a chest X-ray, confirmed by a CT scan.
He has not experienced pain or shortness of breath.
He is a former smoker, having stopped 7 years ago, and has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with medication.
He has a history of smoking, having quit 7 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain, shortness of breath, or other symptoms.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/76, heart rate 70, respiration rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, and the patient is afebrile.
Physical exam: The lungs are clear and equal bilateral, the heart is regular rate and rhythm, and there are no clicks, rubs, or murmurs.
The skin is pink, warm, and dry, and there is no edema to the lower extremities.
The patient has some swelling to the knuckles on each hand, which may be related to his rheumatoid arthritis, but pulses are intact and capillary refill is brisk.
Test Results: A chest CT scan showed a solitary 2-centimeter nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which appears smooth in appearance and without evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule, which is a solitary 2-centimeter nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe.
Problem list: Lung nodule; Rheumatoid Arthritis; Smoking History.
Plan:
I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for the patient's lung function.
I will schedule a PET CT scan to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
I will recommend a video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule, which will involve three small incisions on the side of the right chest and general anesthesia.
The patient will be under observation for one night in the hospital and discharged the following day.
I will also instruct the patient to continue following up with his rheumatologist and adhere to his current medication therapy.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen in a few minutes to sign paperwork and begin the treatment process.
